note_data.title: The Allocation of Resources
keyPoints: **Demand** is the quantity of a good or service that consumers are willing and able to buy at different prices during a given time period
**Supply** is the quantity of a good or service that producers are willing and able to sell at different prices during a given time period
**Market equilibrium** occurs where demand equals supply, determining the market price and quantity sold
**Price elasticity of demand** measures the responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in price
When demand is **price elastic**, percentage change in quantity demanded is greater than percentage change in price
When demand is **price inelastic**, percentage change in quantity demanded is less than percentage change in price
**Income elasticity of demand** measures the responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in consumer income
**Cross elasticity of demand** measures the responsiveness of quantity demanded for one good to a change in price of another good
**Price elasticity of supply** measures the responsiveness of quantity supplied to a change in price

definitions.definition: The quantity of a good or service that consumers are willing and able to buy at different prices during a given time period
The quantity of a good or service that producers are willing and able to sell at different prices during a given time period
The point where demand equals supply, determining the market price and quantity traded
A measure of the responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in price
Goods that can be used instead of each other to satisfy the same want

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show all working clearly. Use the elasticity formula and substitute values
Use cause and effect chains. Link concepts together with 'therefore' or 'as a result'

modelAnswer.question: Calculate the price elasticity of demand if the price of rice in Pakistan increases from Rs.80 per kg to Rs.100 per kg and quantity demanded falls from 500kg to 400kg per week.
modelAnswer.answer: PED = % change in quantity demanded ÷ % change in price. % change in quantity demanded = (400-500)/500 × 100 = -20%. % change in price = (100-80)/80 × 100 = 25%. PED = -20% ÷ 25% = -0.8. The demand for rice is price inelastic because PED is less than 1 in absolute terms.

commonMistakes: Never say 'demand increases' when price falls — always say 'quantity demanded increases'
Students often forget to include 'willing AND able' in demand/supply definitions
Never ignore the negative sign in PED calculations — always interpret the absolute value
Students confuse movement along curves with shifts of curves
Never say elasticity measures 'how much' demand changes — always say 'responsiveness' or 'sensitivity'
examinerTips: Elasticity calculations appear frequently in Paper 2 — always show full working
Always draw and label diagrams for supply and demand questions
Use specific examples from Pakistan (wheat, rice, textiles) to illustrate concepts
Remember to interpret elasticity values — don't just calculate them
formulas: PED = % change in quantity demanded ÷ % change in price
YED = % change in quantity demanded ÷ % change in income
XED = % change in quantity demanded of good A ÷ % change in price of good B
PES = % change in quantity supplied ÷ % change in price
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Supply and demand curves intersecting at equilibrium point, with price on y-axis and quantity on x-axis. Label curves as S and D, equilibrium point as E, equilibrium price as Pe and equilibrium quantity as Qe.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — they asked students to identify which situation demonstrates price elastic demand from multiple scenarios
O/N 2022 P2 Q4 (8 marks) examined price elasticity of demand for sports stadium seats, requiring calculation and analysis of responsiveness when prices increased 10%
M/J 2023 P2 Q6 (12 marks) assessed students' ability to compare the importance of income elasticity versus cross elasticity of demand for business decision-making
O/N 2022 P1 Q18 (1 mark) tested understanding of factors causing outward shifts in labor demand curves
M/J 2023 P1 Q22 (1 mark) examined effects of market consolidation on price elasticity of demand and long-run average costs

workedExample.question: A sports stadium increases ticket prices by 10%. Demand for cheaper seats falls by 5% while demand for expensive seats falls by 12%. Calculate the price elasticity of demand for both types of seats and explain what this tells us about consumer responsiveness. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Apply PED formula = % change in quantity demanded ÷ % change in price
Step 2: For cheaper seats: PED = -5% ÷ 10% = -0.5 (ignore negative sign)
Step 4: Interpret results: cheaper seats are price inelastic (PED < 1), expensive seats are price elastic (PED > 1)
Step 5: Explain responsiveness: consumers are less responsive to price changes for cheaper seats, more responsive for expensive seats
workedExample.answer: Cheaper seats: PED = 0.5 (price inelastic). Expensive seats: PED = 1.2 (price elastic). This shows consumers are more responsive to price changes for expensive seats, likely because they represent luxury purchases with available substitutes, while cheaper seats are more essential with fewer alternatives.
workedExample.markScheme: 2 marks for correct calculations, 2 marks for identifying elastic/inelastic, 2 marks for explaining consumer responsiveness with reference to substitutes/necessity

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates calculated elasticity correctly but failed to ignore the negative sign when interpreting results' — always state whether demand is elastic or inelastic using the absolute value
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently confused income elasticity with price elasticity of demand' — always check which type of elasticity the question is asking for and use the correct formula

Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates often identified elastic/inelastic correctly but provided no economic explanation' — always explain why demand might be elastic/inelastic using factors like substitutes, necessity, time period, and proportion of income
note_data.keyDiagram: Price Elasticity of Demand curve comparison: Draw two demand curves on the same axes with Price (P) on y-axis and Quantity (Q) on x-axis. Show a steep demand curve labeled 'Price Inelastic Demand (PED < 1)' and a flatter demand curve labeled 'Price Elastic Demand (PED > 1)'. Mark identical price changes (P1 to P2) on both curves and show the resulting quantity changes (Q1 to Q2) are smaller for the inelastic curve and larger for the elastic curve. Include labels showing the percentage changes and PED calculations.
note_data.quickRecap: Price elasticity of demand (PED) measures consumer responsiveness to price changes using the formula: % change in quantity demanded ÷ % change in price. PED < 1 means inelastic (consumers less responsive), PED > 1 means elastic (consumers more responsive). Key factors affecting PED include availability of substitutes, whether the good is a necessity or luxury, time period for adjustment, and proportion of income spent on the good.
